title: Fork first-party skills into Claude/Codex/Cursor runtime variants
description:
## Summary

Split the 16 first-party skills under `skills/` into runtime-specific instruction trees (Claude / Codex / Cursor) while sharing scripts, templates, and tests. Each runtime loads only its own `SKILL.md` and metadata — no `Platform — …` blocks or cross-runtime guardrails.

Today every skill is copied once to `~/.skill-symlinks/skills/<name>/` and symlinked identically into `~/.agents`, `~/.claude`, and `~/.cursor`. This pollutes context with instructions for other runtimes (GitHub connector vs `gh`, Claude `Agent`/`AskUserQuestion`, Codex `$skill` chaining, etc.).

**Plan doc (local, to land in first PR):** `plans/runtime-fork-skills.md` — reviewed to 9/10 via plan-with-review.

## Goal

- One logical skill name, three runtime-specific staged trees
- Shared assets in `skills/<name>/shared/`
- Runtime overlays in `skills/<name>/runtimes/{claude,codex,cursor}/`
- Installer keeps one TUI row per skill; assembles and routes per target

## Non-goals (v1)

- Fork third-party skills (`review-loop`, `autoreview`, etc.) — residual chaining pollution documented in `AGENTS.md`; v2 separate effort
- Change skill `name:` or install symlink paths
- Auto-delete legacy staged dirs (`~/.skill-symlinks/skills/<name>/`) without an explicit future flag
- Rewrite bash spec for forked behavior — legacy `test-skills-tui-parity.sh` fixtures stay unforked; fork covered by Go-only E2E

## Target repo layout

```
skills/<name>/
  SKILL.md              # legacy portable; ignored when Forked
  shared/               # scripts, templates, reference docs
  runtimes/
    claude/SKILL.md
    codex/SKILL.md
    codex/agents/openai.yaml
    cursor/SKILL.md
```

`Forked=true` when all three overlay `SKILL.md` files exist. Partial `runtimes/` → WARN, treat as legacy portable.

## Staged install

| Target | Runtime | Staged path | Install root |
|--------|---------|-------------|--------------|
| agents | codex | `~/.skill-symlinks/runtimes/codex/skills/<name>/` | `~/.agents/skills/<name>` |
| claude | claude | `~/.skill-symlinks/runtimes/claude/skills/<name>/` | `~/.claude/skills/<name>` |
| cursor | cursor | `~/.skill-symlinks/runtimes/cursor/skills/<name>/` | `~/.cursor/skills/<name>` |

Assembly: `copy(shared/) + mergeOverlayTree(runtimes/<runtime>/)` → staged tree.

## Installer changes (`tools/skills-tui/internal/skills/`)

Key contracts (full spec in `plans/runtime-fork-skills.md`):

1. **`LinkPath` owned-sources** — extend with `ownedSources ...string` so legacy staged symlinks (`~/.skill-symlinks/skills/<name>`) repoint to per-runtime paths without `--force`. Same owned set on `UnlinkOwned`.

2. **`InstallSkill` per-link sync** — legacy: sync once before loop. Forked: per-link `targetState(l)` → conditional `SyncAssembledStagedSource`; `LinkPath` on all links always.

3. **`pathsMatchAssembled`** — forked freshness compare (shared + overlay vs staged).

4. **`Skill.Forked bool`**, **`Link.CompareShared` / `Link.CompareOverlay`**, **`targetState` forked branch**.

5. **Legacy fallback** — unmigrated skills keep current single-staged behavior.

## TDD slices (dependency order)

| Slice | Focus |
|-------|-------|
| 0 | Parity decision doc |
| 1a | Keyed `Skill` literals (compile) |
| 1b | Discover forked layout |
| 2a | `pathsMatchAssembled` + `mergeOverlayTree` |
| 2b | `SyncAssembledStagedSource` |
| 3 | `StagedSourceForTarget` + `targetRuntime` map |
| 4 | Per-target `SkillLinks` |
| 4b | Per-link `InstallSkill` loop |
| 5 | Legacy portable tests unchanged |
| 6 | Legacy staged-path migration (no `--force`) |
| 7 | `SkillState` aggregation |
| 7b | Mixed stale + foreign apply |
| 8 | `SKILL_INSTALL_TARGETS` subset |
| 9 | Uninstall forked skill |
| 10 | Backup paths |
| 11 | Go-only E2E |
| 12 | Content pilot: `commit` skill |

## Content migration tiers

- **A:** commit, chrome-reading-list, tdd, docs, tdd-with-review, skill-parity-audit, slice-issues
- **B:** ship, fix-pr, autofix, work-prs, merge-prs-review-loop
- **C:** plan-with-review, planned-implementation-agent, product-manager
- **D:** autobuild (Claude full + Codex/Cursor stubs; keep `allow_implicit_invocation: false`)

Pilot PR: installer + `commit`. Follow-up PR: `product-manager`.

## Phases

0. Parity doc + open decisions (Cursor ≈ Claude delegation; no auto-GC)
1. Installer core (slices 1–5, 8) — ~5–7 days
2. Migration/uninstall/backup (slices 6–10) — ~3 days
3. Go E2E (slice 11)
4. Tier A content
5. Tier B (+ `test-fix-pr.sh`)
6. Tier C (`product-manager` separate PR)
7. Tier D + `AGENTS.md` staging table

## Verification (every installer PR)

```bash
scripts/test-skills-tui-go.sh
scripts/test-skills-tui.sh
scripts/test-install-skills.sh
scripts/test-skills-tui-parity.sh      # legacy paths; must stay green
scripts/test-forked-skills-layout.sh   # new
scripts/test-forked-skills-install.sh  # new E2E
scripts/test-fix-pr.sh                 # after Tier B
```

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Forked skill produces three distinct staged trees; legacy skills unchanged
- [ ] Existing users migrate from old staged path without `--force`
- [ ] No `Platform —` blocks remain in migrated first-party skills
- [ ] Codex/Cursor overlays contain no Claude-only API names (CI lint)
- [ ] `test-skills-tui-parity.sh` stays green on legacy fixtures
- [ ] `AGENTS.md` staging table updated

## Open decisions (resolve in Phase 0)

1. Cursor delegation default: mirror Claude (`Task` subagents, `AskQuestion` MCP) — recommended
2. Legacy staged orphan cleanup: document manual; optional `--gc-legacy-staged` in future PR

title: Modernize product-manager skill with current Claude Code features
description:
Modernize `skills/product-manager/` to use current Claude Code features: background/named research agents with SendMessage deep-dives, AskUserQuestion checkpoints, an opt-in Artifact rendering of the brief, a Workflow-based "thorough" research mode, Explore delegation for Phase 1, mechanical read-only enforcement via `disallowed-tools`, plus three small fixes (hardcoded year, `$ARGUMENTS` focus scoping, deferred-tool loading note).

All changes are markdown edits across the three skill files plus one convention touch-up in `AGENTS.md` — no code, so TDD doesn't apply; verification is a manual smoke pass.

## Design decisions

1. **`disallowed-tools` vs. Artifact rendering.** The Artifact flow requires writing an HTML file to the scratchpad, so `Write` cannot be blocked. Resolution: `disallowed-tools: Edit, NotebookEdit` — mechanically blocks file mutation, while project-directory `Write` and git mutations stay covered by the prose HARD-GATE. Codex ignores unknown frontmatter and falls back to prose-only (status quo).
2. **Frontmatter minimalism convention.** `AGENTS.md` says portable frontmatter is `name` + `description` only. We're adding `argument-hint` and `disallowed-tools`, so amend the convention to permit optional Claude-only fields that degrade gracefully when other runtimes ignore them.
3. **The skill must stay inline** (no `context: fork`) — AskUserQuestion doesn't work in forked/subagent contexts, and the checkpoints are the backbone of the skill. Add a one-line note in the skill so a future editor doesn't "optimize" it into a fork.

## File 1: `skills/product-manager/SKILL.md`

### 1a. Frontmatter

```yaml
---
name: product-manager
description: (unchanged)
argument-hint: "[optional focus: competitors | trends | pain-points | distribution]"
disallowed-tools: Edit, NotebookEdit
---
```

### 1b. New "Scope" section (after the announce line)

Handles the `$ARGUMENTS` small fix:

- `$ARGUMENTS` empty → full six-phase pipeline (current behavior, unchanged default).
- Focus dimension given (e.g. `/product-manager competitors`) → lightweight Phase 1 (enough context to brief the researcher), only the matching research pass from Phase 2, focused analysis for that dimension instead of the full brief. Skip Phases 4–5 unless the focus naturally produces them.
- Unrecognized argument text → treat as free-form emphasis ("pay special attention to X") on the full pipeline.

### 1c. HARD-GATE rewording

Keep all never-modify/never-commit language verbatim. Replace the last constraint:

> **NEVER create or modify files in the project directory.** Your deliverable is presented in chat. The only permitted file output is a rendered Artifact of the final brief (Claude Code only, user-accepted, built from a file in the session scratchpad — never in the project).

### 1d. Phase 1 — Explore delegation + structured checkpoint

Add platform blocks after the existing exploration steps:

- **Platform — Claude Code:** Delegate the codebase survey to one `Explore` agent (or two for large repos: "architecture & maturity" / "distribution & CI signals") and keep only the conclusions in the main context; the six identification bullets become the Explore agent's report format. For the checkpoint, present the summary in chat, then use `AskUserQuestion` — single question, options like "Correct — proceed" / "Mostly right (I'll add notes)" / "Off-base — let me redirect", with business context (goals, users, revenue) gathered via the notes/Other path.
- **Platform — Codex:** explore inline and ask the checkpoint question in chat (current behavior).

### 1e. Phase 2 — the biggest rewrite

Replace the current two platform paragraphs with:

**Platform — Claude Code**, two modes:

- **Standard mode (default):** Launch all four research agents **in a single message** so they run concurrently, as background agents with fixed names: `competitor-research`, `market-trends`, `pain-points`, `distribution-channels`. Each prompt built from `<skill-dir>/research-agent.md`. Note explicitly: these names matter — Phase 6 deep-dives continue them via `SendMessage` instead of re-researching. Deferred-tools fix: if researching inline instead (user declined delegation), load `WebSearch` and `WebFetch` in **one** `ToolSearch` call before starting.
- **Workflow mode (thorough):** Trigger when the user asks for a thorough/comprehensive/deep analysis or says "workflow mode." Use the `Workflow` tool (skill instruction = legitimate opt-in):
  - `parallel()` fan-out: one `agent()` per dimension, each with a **JSON schema** for findings (schema defined in `research-agent.md`).
  - Second stage: adversarial **verification pass** on time-sensitive claims — pricing, funding, market size, download counts. One verifier per flagged claim, prompted to refute; unverified claims get marked `confidence: low` rather than dropped.
  - Include a compact workflow script sketch in the skill (meta block + two phases: Research, Verify) so the model doesn't have to invent the orchestration.
  - Note the cost honestly: this spawns 10–20 agents; only use when asked.
  - Caveat to encode: workflow agents aren't persistent, so Phase 6 deep-dives after workflow mode spawn fresh agents instead of using SendMessage.

**Platform — Codex:** unchanged (inline unless a documented safe subagent mechanism exists).

Keep the "read findings, discard filler" paragraph — it applies to both modes.

### 1f. Phase 4 checkpoint — AskUserQuestion multiSelect

- **Platform — Claude Code:** after presenting the ranked list in chat, use `AskUserQuestion` with **multiSelect** questions asking "Which features resonate?" Max 4 options per question and 4 questions per call, so chunk the 5–10 features 4-per-question (feature name as label, one-line why as description). Re-rank based on selections and notes.
- **Platform — Codex:** ask in chat (current behavior).

### 1g. Phase 6 — Artifact offer + deep-dive routing

- After presenting the brief in chat (chat delivery stays the default and always happens), **Platform — Claude Code:** offer to render the brief as an Artifact. If accepted: load the `artifact-design` skill first (required), write HTML to the **scratchpad** (never the project), call `Artifact` with a stable title ("Product Brief: <app>") and favicon `📊`, structure mirroring `product-brief-template.md`, theme-aware, fully self-contained.
- Deep-dive follow-ups: **Platform — Claude Code, standard mode:** `SendMessage` to the named researcher whose dimension matches ("go deeper on competitor X's pricing" → `competitor-research`). Workflow mode or Codex: spawn/run a fresh focused research pass.

### 1h. Common Mistakes + Red Flags additions

- Mistakes table, two rows: "Re-researching from scratch for a deep-dive when a named researcher is still alive → SendMessage it" and "Rendering an Artifact without offering first / writing brief files into the project → chat is the default deliverable; Artifact is opt-in, scratchpad-only."
- Red Flags, one bullet: "You are about to write a file anywhere other than the session scratchpad."

## File 2: `skills/product-manager/research-agent.md`

- **Year fix:** `"[category] tools 2026"` → `"[category] tools [current year]"`, and same for `"[category] market 2026"`.
- **New "Structured Output (Workflow mode)" section** at the end: JSON schema per dimension for Workflow `agent()` calls. Shared shape: `findings[]` with `{claim, detail, source_url, confidence, time_sensitive}`, plus per-dimension fields (competitors: `{name, url, positioning, pricing_model, strengths, weaknesses, market_signals}`; etc.). `time_sensitive: true` feeds the verification stage. The existing markdown output format stays as-is for standard mode, with one sentence clarifying which mode uses which.

## File 3: `skills/product-manager/product-brief-template.md`

Minimal touch: add an optional **"Verification notes"** line under Sources (used in workflow mode: which claims were adversarially verified vs. taken from a single source), and a one-line header note that the Artifact rendering mirrors this structure section-for-section.

## File 4: `AGENTS.md` (repo root; `CLAUDE.md` is a symlink so no separate edit)

Amend the conventions bullet:

> Keep portable skill frontmatter minimal: `name` and `description`. Optional Claude-only fields (`argument-hint`, `disallowed-tools`) are acceptable when the skill degrades gracefully on runtimes that ignore them.

No skill-inventory changes needed (no new skill).

## Execution order & git workflow

1. Pull latest `main`, branch `product-manager-modernize` (never commit to main).
2. Commit in three logical chunks:
   - `SKILL.md` — modes, checkpoints, artifact, scope/frontmatter.
   - `research-agent.md` + `product-brief-template.md` — year fix, schema section, template notes.
   - `AGENTS.md` convention amendment.
3. Verification (manual, since skills have no test harness):
   - YAML frontmatter parses (`/reload-skills` in a session; confirm skill still loads and lists).
   - Confirm no Codex-only breakage: platform blocks correctly labeled, no `$skill` chaining in prose, `<skill-dir>` used for the template references.
   - Smoke run: invoke `/product-manager` against a small repo in a throwaway session; confirm the announce line, the Phase 1 AskUserQuestion checkpoint fires, standard mode launches four named agents in one message, and nothing writes outside the scratchpad. (Workflow mode smoke is optional given cost — reading the generated script before approving is enough.)
4. Ship as a PR once reviewed.

## Resolved default

No `agents/openai.yaml` will be added for Codex UI metadata (the skill doesn't have one today, and nothing here requires it).

title: Split skills for Claude Code vs Codex targeting (runtime self-selection)
description:
## Summary

Rework the first-party skills under `skills/` so a single skill can present **platform-tailored content** to Claude Code vs. Codex, instead of one portable `SKILL.md` whose "neutral" prose leaks Codex-isms and Claude-only assumptions. Most skills stay single-source; only the genuinely divergent ones carry a short per-platform block that the **agent self-selects at runtime**. No installer changes.

The split targets each runtime's **execution model**, not just install paths.

## Motivation

Every first-party skill today is one portable `SKILL.md` (minimal `name`/`description` frontmatter) plus `agents/openai.yaml` for Codex UI metadata. That splits the *launcher chrome* but not the *behavior*. Several skills describe mechanics that execute differently per runtime, papered over with neutral-sounding prose:

- `$review-loop` / `$autoreview` / `$commit` chaining is a Codex-native prefix convention; Claude invokes skills via the Skill tool.
- Delegation is **asymmetric**: Claude Code's `Agent`/`Task` tool is effectively always present ("spawn a reviewer subagent"); Codex's multi-agent tooling is discovered via `tool_search` and may be absent, needing an inline fallback that discloses the limitation.
- Hardcoded `~/.claude/skills/...` paths (e.g. `chrome-reading-list`, `autobuild`) break on the agents/Codex root `~/.agents/skills`.
- GitHub access policy differs: Codex should prefer installed connectors with `gh` as fallback; Claude stays `gh`/CLI-first.
- `commit` defaults new branches to `codex/<topic>` even under Claude.
- `autobuild` is hardwired to `claude -p` + Claude permission flags (`--permission-mode`, `--allowedTools`) and cannot serve Codex without a different driver.

## Three-layer model

Separate every skill into three layers and only fork the ones that must:

1. **Domain workflow** — what good work looks like. Shared.
2. **Platform mechanics** — subagent invocation, skill chaining, tool discovery (`tool_search`), connectors vs `gh`, permissions, `claude -p` vs `codex exec`. Forks where it diverges.
3. **Install/runtime paths** — `~/.claude/skills` vs `~/.agents/skills`, bundled script location. Referenced as `<skill-dir>` in prose, never hardcoded.

## Divergence axes

| Axis | Claude Code | Codex (agents root) |
|---|---|---|
| Skill chaining | Skill tool / slash command | `$name` prefix |
| Delegation | `Agent`/`Task` tool assumed present; can fan out in parallel | discover via `tool_search`; if absent, run inline and disclose |
| GitHub access | `gh`/CLI-first | installed connector preferred, `gh` fallback |
| Headless driver | `claude -p`, `--permission-mode`, `--allowedTools` | `codex exec`, sandbox/approval policy |
| Install root | `~/.claude/skills/<name>` | `~/.agents/skills/<name>` |
| UI metadata | frontmatter `description` | `agents/openai.yaml` (already handled) |

## Chosen approach: runtime self-selection, tiered by divergence

Ship both platforms' guidance in one `SKILL.md` and let the running agent follow only the block for its own runtime. **No install-time stripping, no two-variant staging, no installer changes.** Every skill keeps its single staged copy and today's symlink behavior is unchanged.

There is a partial precedent: `agents/openai.yaml` already ships in every first-party skill and only Codex reads it (Claude ignores it). That establishes that Codex-specific content is *safe* to carry in the tree — but note the mechanism differs: `openai.yaml` is partitioned **by file** (Codex opens it, Claude never does), whereas self-selection asks **both runtimes to read the same `SKILL.md`** and each skip the other's block. The self-selection argument stands on its own — each runtime reliably knows its identity — not solely on this analogy.

Do **not** apply a uniform overlay to all 16 skills — most diverge by one line. Tier by actual divergence:

- **Tier N — Neutral (no per-platform block):** scrub Codex-isms in place. Replace `$skill` with "run the *X* skill (invoke it if available; otherwise follow the inline section below)"; `~/.claude/...` → `<skill-dir>`; drop the `codex/<topic>` branch default; add the connector-vs-`gh` policy line where GitHub is touched. Where a skill leans on MCP/connector tools, the Codex side can additionally declare them in `agents/openai.yaml` under `dependencies.tools` (advisory, not a permission boundary).
- **Tier P — Per-platform block:** one `SKILL.md` with a short labeled block for each runtime on the diverging section only. The agent reads both and applies its own.
- **Tier X — True fork:** `autobuild` only. Decide Claude-only vs. a real `codex exec` sibling (runtime decision, not doc edits). `chrome-reading-list` is *not* a fork — it only needs a portable path.

### Per-platform block convention

Plain, readable Markdown — the reader is the model, so no machine delimiters are needed:

```
When delegating review:

**Platform — Claude Code:** Spawn a fresh-context reviewer with the `Agent`
tool; independent reviewers may run in parallel.

**Platform — Codex:** Discover multi-agent tooling with `tool_search`. If a
reviewer agent is available, dispatch it; otherwise run the review inline in
the main context and state that no separate reviewer was used.
```

Plus a single standing line near the top of any multi-platform skill:

> This skill has sections labeled **Platform — <name>**. Follow only the block for the runtime you are; ignore the others.

Keep forked blocks short. A `<!-- platform:x -->` machine tag can be layered on later *if* an optional stripper is ever wanted; it is out of scope now.

## Per-skill classification

| Skill | Tier | Note |
|---|---|---|
| `autobuild` | X | `claude -p`, `~/.claude/skills`, Claude permission flags. Claude-only or a `codex exec` sibling. Do last. |
| `planned-implementation-agent` | P | Worker/reviewer subagent dispatch differs (assumed vs discover-or-inline). |
| `plan-with-review` | P | Separate reviewer subagent; **pilot target**. |
| `merge-prs-review-loop` | P | Reviewer subagents + review-loop chaining + GitHub access policy. |
| `product-manager` | P | Already says "current Codex environment"; give Claude vs Codex blocks (subagents vs connector/discovery). |
| `chrome-reading-list` | N | Hardcoded `~/.claude/skills/...extract.py` path → portable `<skill-dir>`. Also reconcile its Claude-only `user_invocable: true` frontmatter with Codex's `policy.allow_implicit_invocation` in `openai.yaml` ("similar intent, not equivalent semantics"). |
| `skill-parity-audit` | N | Default command root differs; label by host. |
| `autofix` | N | `$autoreview`/`$ship`/`$tdd` chaining + connector-vs-`gh`. |
| `fix-pr` | N | Delegates to `$autofix`; clarify invoke-skill vs script. |
| `tdd-with-review` | N | Composition only; chaining phrasing. |
| `ship` | N | GitHub PR tool guidance (connector vs `gh`). |
| `work-prs` | N | Mostly `gh`; add connector option for Codex. |
| `docs` | N | Keep `CLAUDE.md` symlink rule as a **repo convention** (shared, not a Claude fork). |
| `commit` | N | Drop `codex/<topic>` default → neutral `agent/<topic>`/descriptive. |
| `slice-issues` | N | Connector mention for Codex; `gh`-first for Claude. |
| `tdd` | N | Nearly neutral; light scrub only. |

## Implementation phases

All tiers are now pure documentation edits — no installer/shell work, no test-harness changes.

**Phase 1 — Pilot one Tier-P skill (`plan-with-review`).**
Add the per-platform delegation block (Claude: `Agent` tool; Codex: `tool_search` discover-or-inline) + the standing self-select line. Confirm both blocks read clearly and the convention feels right before rolling out.

**Phase 2 — Convention + docs.**
Document the tiers, the **Platform — <name>** block convention, and the self-select instruction in `AGENTS.md` (Conventions). Note that `<skill-dir>` replaces hardcoded install roots.

**Phase 3 — Remaining Tier-P skills.**
`planned-implementation-agent`, `merge-prs-review-loop`, `product-manager` — same block pattern for delegation + chaining + GitHub-access divergence.

**Phase 4 — Tier-N sweep.**
The 10 neutral skills: `$skill` → "run the *X* skill…", de-hardcode paths, connector-vs-`gh` policy line, drop `codex/` default, keep `docs` symlink rule as repo convention. Independent of the other phases; can ship first if preferred.

**Phase 5 — `autobuild` decision.**
Separate follow-up: pick Claude-only vs. `codex exec` sibling. Out of scope for the doc-level split; tracked here as a known fork.

## Testing

- No installer/staging tests needed (behavior unchanged).
- Optional lightweight check: a lint/grep ensuring every `**Platform — Claude Code:**` block in a skill has a matching `**Platform — Codex:**` block (and vice versa) so a platform is never silently omitted.
- Manual: read the pilot `SKILL.md` as each runtime and confirm the correct block is followed.

## Reference: current Codex skill schema

Confirmed against https://developers.openai.com/codex/skills (fetched 2026-07-01):

- Skill layout (`SKILL.md` required; `scripts/`, `references/`, `assets/`, `agents/openai.yaml` optional) and `name`/`description`-only frontmatter match this repo's convention exactly.
- Full `agents/openai.yaml` schema — the repo currently uses only a subset (`interface.display_name`, `interface.short_description`, `interface.default_prompt`):
  - `interface`: `display_name`, `short_description`, `default_prompt`, plus optional `icon_small`, `icon_large`, `brand_color`.
  - `policy.allow_implicit_invocation` (default `true`) — whether Codex may auto-select the skill from its `description`.
  - `dependencies.tools[]` — declare MCP/connector tools (`type`, `value`, `description`, `transport`, `url`); advisory, not a permission boundary.
- Invocation: explicit via `/skills` or `$skill-name`; implicit when `description` matches (gated by `allow_implicit_invocation`). This validates keeping `$name` as the user-facing invocation while neutralizing in-body skill chaining.

## Risks & open decisions

- **Agent picks the wrong block:** low — each runtime knows its own identity, and the standing self-select line makes the rule explicit. Keeping blocks short and adjacent minimizes confusion.
- **Both blocks always in context:** negligible token cost for short forked sections; acceptable given the machinery it removes.
- **Codex delegation reality:** confirm whether Codex subagents are always present or genuinely discovered — determines whether the Codex block needs the inline fallback or can assume the primitive. (Working assumption: discover-or-fallback.)
- **`~/.agents` is generic, not Codex-only:** the "Codex" block label is shorthand for "agents-family root"; wording should reflect that where it matters.
- **Superseded alternative:** an earlier draft proposed install-time stripping (two staged variants + a `strip_platform` filter in `scripts/skills-tui.sh`). Dropped — it added the only data-safety-sensitive code in the plan to hide text the agent can simply skip itself.

## Out of scope

- Third-party skills under `third-party/` (separate audit).
- Claude-native `agent-teams/` assets (stay Claude-only).
- The `autobuild` runtime rewrite (Phase 5 is a decision stub only).
- An install-time platform stripper (only if a future need appears).

title: Fix Codex session archive metadata/transcript ID mismatches
description:
## Problem

Some saved Codex session archives under `~/.agent-sessions/codex` have valid files but inconsistent session identity bookkeeping: the archive directory and `metadata.json` claim one session ID, while the copied `transcript.jsonl` starts with a different `session_meta.payload.id`.

This makes the archive look structurally healthy while pointing at the wrong underlying transcript.

## Evidence

Recent archive check:

```text
archive                              metadata_id                          transcript_id                        result
019ede32-a078-7fd2-ad13-3e072e9b2849 019ede32-a078-7fd2-ad13-3e072e9b2849 019ede32-a078-7fd2-ad13-3e072e9b2849 OK
019ede30-0572-7153-bc19-049f93a156f6 019ede30-0572-7153-bc19-049f93a156f6 019ede30-0572-7153-bc19-049f93a156f6 OK
019ede2b-3349-73f2-a644-3f490bba194c 019ede2b-3349-73f2-a644-3f490bba194c 019ede2b-3349-73f2-a644-3f490bba194c OK
019ede2c-4a46-7001-9940-10514ef9dbf1 019ede2c-4a46-7001-9940-10514ef9dbf1 019ede2b-3349-73f2-a644-3f490bba194c MISMATCH
019ede1c-3833-7fc0-b03e-a328ea9b3190 019ede1c-3833-7fc0-b03e-a328ea9b3190 019ede1b-89fa-7bd0-9771-8751d230a631 MISMATCH
```

Full archive scan found `4` mismatches out of `1764` saved sessions:

```text
019eddf8-bc7c-7b33-8689-9537fabaa7ed metadata=019eddf8-bc7c-7b33-8689-9537fabaa7ed transcript=019eddf7-9548-75c3-9fb1-4415027459db
019ede09-e0c1-7320-b5e3-4470e0657426 metadata=019ede09-e0c1-7320-b5e3-4470e0657426 transcript=019ede09-1d70-7a43-bdd2-9cf922b86ca6
019ede1c-3833-7fc0-b03e-a328ea9b3190 metadata=019ede1c-3833-7fc0-b03e-a328ea9b3190 transcript=019ede1b-89fa-7bd0-9771-8751d230a631
019ede2c-4a46-7001-9940-10514ef9dbf1 metadata=019ede2c-4a46-7001-9940-10514ef9dbf1 transcript=019ede2b-3349-73f2-a644-3f490bba194c
```

The files are otherwise healthy:

- `metadata.json` parses as valid JSON.
- `transcript.jsonl` parses line-by-line as valid JSON.
- `metadata.transcript_lines` and `metadata.transcript_bytes` match the actual archived transcript.
- Recent `save-session.log` entries are `OK`, `copied=true`.

## Suspected cause

The active hook is installed as:

```text
~/.codex/hooks/save-session.sh -> /Users/brian/dev/agent-skills-codex-hook/hooks/save-codex-session/save-session.sh
```

In `hooks/save-codex-session/save-session.sh`, the script keeps the hook payload `session_id` when a transcript is found, even if the transcript's internal `session_meta.payload.id` differs. In fallback paths such as transcript lookup by cwd or stale/misleading payload path, this can create an archive directory and metadata for one ID while copying a transcript for another.

Relevant behavior:

```bash
meta_sid="$(transcript_meta "$transcript" '.id')"
[ -n "$session_id" ] || session_id="$meta_sid"
```

That only fills `session_id` when empty; it does not reject or repair a mismatch.

## Expected behavior

The archive directory name, `metadata.json.session.session_id`, and `transcript.jsonl` `session_meta.payload.id` should always agree when a transcript is copied.

If the payload session ID and transcript session ID disagree, the hook should do one of these deliberately:

1. Prefer an exact transcript match for the payload session ID.
2. If using a fallback transcript, adopt the transcript's own session ID for the destination and metadata.
3. Refuse to copy and log a warning/error instead of writing a misleading archive.

## Suggested acceptance checks

- Add a test/fixture where the hook payload has `session_id=A` but fallback transcript has `session_meta.payload.id=B`.
- Verify the hook does not produce an archive at `A` containing transcript `B`.
- Add a validation command or script that checks archive dir ID == metadata ID == transcript ID across `~/.agent-sessions/codex`.
- Consider logging explicit mismatch warnings with both IDs and the selected transcript path.

title: Build skill importer TUI
description:
# PRD: Skill Importer TUI

## Problem Statement

Personal skills are currently managed by hand across Claude Code and Codex. Installed skills may be canonical local skills, imported experiments, symlinks, broken symlinks, or external directories. The user has to remember where each agent looks for skills, manually create or remove symlinks, inspect skill frontmatter by hand, and decide when an imported skill should become part of the canonical local skill collection.

This creates friction when evaluating skills from other repositories, direct skill file links, local paths, or pasted Markdown. The current workflow also makes it easy to accidentally overwrite a real directory, remove the wrong symlink, lose track of imported source metadata, or promote something before it has been tested. The user needs one small terminal-native tool that makes the current skill landscape visible and lets them import, enable, disable, and promote skills deliberately.

## Solution

Build a Rust terminal UI named `skill-importer` that is the primary interface from day one. The TUI shows all discovered skills across Claude Code and Codex, including canonical local skills and unpromoted imports. It lets the user paste Markdown, enter a direct skill file URL, enter a repository URL, or enter a local path to import a skill. Imported skills are stored locally in a dedicated imports area and are enabled per agent by creating symlinks into the selected agent's skill root.

Promotion copies an imported skill into the canonical local skill collection and rewrites any enabled symlinks to point at the promoted canonical skill. Promotion targets both ecosystems conceptually because canonical skills are portable, but enablement remains per-agent: a skill can be enabled for Claude Code only, Codex only, both, or neither. Promotion must not edit documentation or installer scripts; those updates will be managed separately.

Repository imports must support interactive selection when a repository contains multiple skills. The tool should keep its core behavior independent from terminal rendering so discovery, import parsing, symlink management, promotion, and TUI state can all be tested with behavior-focused tests.

## User Stories

1. As a skill author, I want to see every Claude Code skill in one list, so that I know what Claude Code can currently use.
2. As a skill author, I want to see every Codex skill in one list, so that I know what Codex can currently use.
3. As a skill author, I want one merged list across both agents, so that duplicate skills are not mentally counted twice.
4. As a skill author, I want to see whether each skill is canonical, imported, external, or broken, so that I understand what kind of object I am acting on.
5. As a skill author, I want to see whether a skill is enabled for Claude Code, Codex, both, or neither, so that I can control agent-specific availability.
6. As a skill author, I want to filter the skill list, so that I can find a specific skill quickly.
7. As a skill author, I want to inspect a skill's name and description, so that I can verify it is the skill I meant to manage.
8. As a skill author, I want broken symlinks to be visible instead of ignored, so that I can clean up bad installs deliberately.
9. As a skill author, I want external symlinks to be marked clearly, so that I do not accidentally replace something managed outside this tool.
10. As a skill author, I want real directories in agent skill roots to be protected from deletion, so that disabling a skill never destroys local work.
11. As a skill author, I want to paste skill Markdown into the TUI, so that I can import a skill without creating a file first.
12. As a skill author, I want to import a direct skill file URL, so that I can try a skill from a raw file link.
13. As a skill author, I want to import a repository URL, so that I can evaluate skills published in another repository.
14. As a skill author, I want to import a local path, so that I can test a skill from another checkout on my machine.
15. As a skill author, I want imports to validate minimal skill frontmatter, so that malformed skills fail before they are enabled.
16. As a skill author, I want validation errors to name the missing or invalid field, so that I can fix the source quickly.
17. As a skill author, I want imported skills stored locally before promotion, so that experiments are separated from canonical skills.
18. As a skill author, I want imported skills to keep source metadata, so that I can remember where they came from.
19. As a skill author, I want imported skills to keep a content hash, so that I can identify changed imports later.
20. As a skill author, I want importing to refuse name collisions by default, so that existing imports and canonical skills are not overwritten.
21. As a skill author, I want repository imports with multiple valid skills to show an interactive selection flow, so that I can choose the skill I actually want.
22. As a skill author, I want repository imports to preserve supporting files, so that skills with references, scripts, or templates still work.
23. As a skill author, I want the TUI to be keyboard-first, so that managing skills feels fast in the terminal.
24. As a skill author, I want clear action hints, so that I can use the TUI without memorizing hidden commands.
25. As a skill author, I want to switch the active enablement target between Claude Code and Codex, so that I can manage each agent independently.
26. As a skill author, I want to enable a selected imported skill for Claude Code, so that Claude Code can use it before it is promoted.
27. As a skill author, I want to enable a selected imported skill for Codex, so that Codex can use it before it is promoted.
28. As a skill author, I want to enable a selected skill for both agents, so that portable skills are available everywhere with one action.
29. As a skill author, I want disabling a skill to remove only managed symlinks, so that unrelated files are never deleted.
30. As a skill author, I want the tool to refuse unsafe symlink replacement, so that external links are not silently overwritten.
31. As a skill author, I want the tool to report every filesystem action it takes, so that I can trust what changed.
32. As a skill author, I want promotion to copy an imported skill into the canonical skill collection, so that accepted skills become part of my durable local library.
33. As a skill author, I want promotion to preserve supporting files, so that promoted skills behave like the imported versions I tested.
34. As a skill author, I want promotion to refuse canonical name collisions, so that existing canonical skills are protected.
35. As a skill author, I want promotion to relink enabled agent installs to the canonical skill, so that enabled skills stop pointing at the imports area.
36. As a skill author, I want promotion to leave documentation and installer scripts alone, so that those changes can be managed in a separate workflow.
37. As a skill author, I want to delete unpromoted imports, so that rejected experiments do not accumulate forever.
38. As a skill author, I want deletion to be unavailable for canonical skills, so that cleanup actions stay scoped to imported experiments.
39. As a skill author, I want non-interactive commands to share the same core behavior, so that tests and automation can exercise the tool without terminal driving.
40. As a skill author, I want JSON output for listing skills, so that future scripts can inspect the same discovery state.
41. As a skill author, I want all paths to be configurable for tests, so that behavior can be verified without touching real agent skill roots.
42. As a skill author, I want network and repository fetching to be isolated from parsing and storage, so that imports can be tested deterministically.
43. As a skill author, I want the app to survive missing skill roots, so that first-time setup does not crash.
44. As a skill author, I want the app to create missing managed roots when enabling a skill, so that the first enable action is smooth.
45. As a skill author, I want a visible success or failure result after each action, so that I never wonder whether an operation completed.

## Implementation Decisions

- The tool will be a Rust terminal UI with the TUI as the primary product interface from the first release.
- The tool will also expose non-interactive commands for listing, importing, enabling, disabling, promoting, and deleting imports, but these commands exist primarily to make behavior testable and automatable.
- The core skill-management behavior will live outside the rendering layer. The rendering layer will consume app state and dispatch actions rather than containing filesystem logic.
- Discovery will merge canonical skills, imported skills, Claude Code skills, and Codex skills into a single skill inventory.
- Discovery will classify target entries as managed symlinks, external symlinks, real directories, missing entries, or broken symlinks.
- Import handling will support pasted Markdown, direct skill file URLs, repository URLs, and local paths.
- Import validation will require minimal portable skill frontmatter with a name and description.
- Imported skills will be stored in a dedicated local imports area with a manifest that records source type, source location, import time, content hash, and promotion status.
- Repository import will detect valid skill directories and provide an interactive selection flow when multiple valid skills are present.
- Repository import will preserve supporting files that belong to the selected skill.
- Enablement will be per-agent. A skill may be enabled for Claude Code, Codex, both, or neither.
- Enablement will be implemented through symlinks from each agent's skill root to either the imported skill or the canonical promoted skill.
- Disabling will remove only managed symlinks and must refuse to delete real directories or unmanaged external symlinks.
- Promotion will copy an imported skill into the canonical local skill collection.
- Promotion will rewrite any managed symlinks for that skill to point at the canonical promoted skill.
- Promotion will not update documentation or installer scripts.
- Promotion will refuse to overwrite an existing canonical skill.
- The app will make filesystem roots configurable so tests can use temporary directories.
- Network fetching and repository fetching will be behind injectable boundaries so import behavior can be tested without depending on live remote resources.
- Errors will be expressed in user-facing language suitable for the TUI status area and for non-interactive command output.

## Testing Decisions

- Good tests for this tool verify externally visible behavior through public interfaces. They should assert discovered skill states, created files, created or removed symlinks, validation errors, imported metadata, promotion results, and app state transitions. They should not assert private helper function structure or terminal drawing internals.
- Discovery will be tested with temporary canonical, import, Claude Code, and Codex roots containing directories, managed symlinks, external symlinks, and broken symlinks.
- Markdown import will be tested by passing valid and invalid skill Markdown through the public import interface and asserting stored import contents and manifest data.
- Direct skill file URL import will be tested through an injectable fetcher that returns deterministic content.
- Repository import will be tested with local test repositories or injectable repository providers, including a repository with exactly one skill and a repository with multiple skills requiring selection.
- Local path import will be tested with temporary directories containing a root skill and a nested skill.
- Enable and disable behavior will be tested by asserting the final filesystem state in temporary agent skill roots.
- Unsafe disable and replacement behavior will be tested by creating real directories and external symlinks and verifying they are not removed.
- Promotion will be tested by importing a skill, enabling it for one or both agents, promoting it, and asserting the canonical copy exists while managed symlinks point to the canonical skill.
- Promotion collision behavior will be tested by creating an existing canonical skill and verifying promotion fails without modifying it.
- TUI app state will be tested through reducer-style state transitions for selection, filtering, target switching, import prompts, multi-skill selection, and action results.
- TUI rendering will receive a smoke test using a terminal test backend to verify the main screen renders key sections and selected skill information without panics.
- Tests should follow a TDD sequence: write one behavior test, implement the minimum code needed to pass, then refactor while green.

## Out of Scope

- Automatically editing documentation during promotion.
- Automatically editing installer scripts during promotion.
- Overwriting existing canonical skills.
- Resolving semantic differences between Claude-native skills and portable skills beyond marking discovered status.
- Publishing imported or promoted skills to a remote repository.
- Updating remote imported skills after their source changes.
- Trust, sandboxing, or security review of third-party skill content beyond basic validation and clear source metadata.
- Full-screen visual snapshot testing for every TUI state.
- Supporting non-local agent ecosystems beyond Claude Code and Codex.

## Further Notes

This feature should be built with TDD from the start. The first tracer bullet should prove that the tool can discover installed skills across configurable temporary roots and report the merged inventory through a public interface. Later slices should add one import source or management operation at a time.

The highest-risk behavior is filesystem mutation. The implementation should be conservative around symlink replacement and deletion, with clear status messages and tests for unsafe existing entries. The second-highest-risk behavior is repository import because repositories may contain zero, one, or many valid skills; interactive selection should be treated as a core part of the full product, not a follow-up.
